summary: Gruber recaps The Talk Show Live from WWDC 2024, recorded at The California Theatre in San Jose with Apple executives John Giannandrea, Craig Federighi, and Greg Joswiak as guests. The centerpiece of the post is the groundbreaking 3D livestream of the event for Vision Pro viewers, an initiative driven entirely by Adam Lisagor and his team at Sandwich, not by Apple. Gruber details the technical setup involving a custom dual-camera rig and spatial audio microphones, and distinguishes the result as '3D video with spatial audio' rather than true spatial video. The post includes extensive positive reactions from social media, with viewers describing the experience as remarkably immersive, like sitting in the front row.
key_points: The 3D livestream for Vision Pro was entirely Adam Lisagor's initiative, pitched to Gruber only eight weeks before the event, not an Apple-driven effort
The technical setup used a custom rig with two Panasonic Lumix BGH1 cameras with Olympus 17mm f/1.2 lenses placed about 3 inches apart, plus spatial audio microphones throughout the theater
Gruber carefully distinguishes '3D video with spatial audio' from true 'spatial video,' noting the latter would be more immersive with full look-around capability
This was believed to be the first event ever livestreamed in 3D for viewing in VisionOS
Social media reactions were unanimously positive, with viewers comparing the experience to actually sitting in the front row
money_quotes: I was like, 'Well, sure, that sounds awesome, but how in the world would we do that? What camera could we use to shoot with? How would we stream it? What app would people be able to view it in? It's a great idea but none of this seems possible.' Adam was like 'I think we can do it.'
And, son of a bitch, they did it.
Apple's reaction echoed my own: sounds great but how?

True spatial video would be more immersive — 'Look left to see Joz, look right to see Gruber' — and the effect we achieved wasn't quite like that. But what we did get is immersive, and very compelling.
To the best of my knowledge, this was the first event livestreamed in 3D for viewing in VisionOS, and while my role was simply as the host of the show, that's pretty damn cool.
Those sort of things just can't be experienced with a TV screen. It all felt like sitting there, and looking into the stage.
key_insight: The first-ever 3D livestream for Vision Pro wasn't an Apple initiative but a scrappy creative effort pulled off in eight weeks by independent filmmakers, demonstrating that compelling spatial content can emerge from outside Apple's own ecosystem.
